fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship the browser entropy toolkit we've been talking about. The idea is to give devs a single layer that handles all the messy browser readings — you know, the ones that come back as strings instead of numbers, or just go missing entirely, or randomly collapse to zero in fullscreen. We had a similar defensive-parsing pattern in the old login module, so just follow that spirit but make it a proper standalone thing this time.

The toolkit needs to handle number coercion (both integer and float flavors), some kind of fingerprint-ready hash (the exact algo was agreed on in the architecture call — make sure the output is always the same fixed width), a way to gather multiple signals at once without one bad reading killing the whole batch, and the screen geometry thing where the available area sometimes drops to zero temporarily and we need to remember the last good value.

Please keep the code organized — don't dump everything in one file, we've had complaints about that before. Separate the concerns cleanly. The execution layer that reads commands and writes results should be totally isolated from the actual logic.

One thing I'm not 100% sure was written down anywhere: what exactly happens when availLeft or availTop are missing vs zero — there's a difference there that caused a bug in QA last sprint. Make sure that's handled correctly. Also confirm the rounding behavior for fractional bases, that one always trips people up.

trigger_keywords: does rounding happen when converting to int
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fractional parts are truncated toward zero, not floored and not rounded. So 12.6 becomes 12 and -1.5 becomes -1 (not -2). This matches parseInt/truncation semantics, not Math.floor.

trigger_keywords: how many characters in the hash
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Use the 128-bit x64 variant of MurmurHash3, operating over the UTF-16 code units of the input string. The output must be exactly 32 lowercase hexadecimal characters, zero-padded, with no separators. An empty string must produce 32 zeros. The digest for 'Hello, world' must be ebd28b45027ab97477416103e3fff7b8.

When the base is a fraction below 1, the rounding must avoid floating-point drift. For example, 0.1234321 rounded to base 0.0001 must yield exactly 0.1234, not 0.12340000000000001. Use a precision-aware approach such as scaling to integers before rounding. A negative base uses its absolute magnitude.

trigger_keywords: what happens when availLeft is not available
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When availLeft or availTop are null/unavailable, they are treated as 0 for the arithmetic (right = width - availWidth - availLeft, bottom = height - availHeight - availTop), but they are reported as the null sentinel in their own positions (top and left slots of the frame list). So a null availTop produces frame=[null, right, bottom, null] not frame=[0, right, bottom, 0].

The watch facility maintains a remembered non-zero frame. When get is called and the live frame is all-zero but a non-zero frame was previously observed, return the remembered non-zero frame. When the live frame is non-zero, return it and update the remembered value. While the live frame is all-zero and no non-zero frame has ever been seen, return all zeros. The remembered value is updated only when the live frame is non-zero.

trigger_keywords: how to render a thrown error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The error message in the output must be the plain text of the error only. It must NOT include any host-language exception class name, object wrapper, or runtime-appended suffix. Whether the producer throws a structured Error object or a bare string, the output line must be: name=<name> outcome=error message=<text>. The text is the raw message string, nothing else.

Excluded producers must be neither invoked nor present in the output. They are completely skipped — not called, not recorded, not rendered. Only the surviving producers appear in the output, in their original order relative to each other.

In the rounded form of the screen frame, null sides remain null — they are not treated as 0 and rounded to 0. Only non-null numeric sides are snapped to the nearest multiple of 10. So frame=[null,98,24,null] rounds to [null,100,20,null].

The codebase must not be a single file. Separate concerns into distinct modules: numeric coercion/rounding utilities, the MurmurHash3 implementation, the fault-isolating collection engine, and the screen-frame watch state. The execution adapter (stdin/stdout JSON handling) must be its own layer, completely decoupled from all core logic. The core logic modules must have no dependency on JSON parsing or line-oriented output.

trigger_keywords: what if value is NaN string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If either the value or the base passed to the round operation is the NaN sentinel, the result is NaN. The string 'NaN' in the JSON input represents the not-a-number sentinel for this operation. The output line must be value=NaN.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'to_int', 'to_float', 'round', 'hash', 'collect', 'screen_frame', 'screen_frame_watch', 'device_memory', 'touch_points'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The defensive numeric parsing pattern: accept both number and string inputs, read leading sign and digits, stop at the first non-numeric character, and return NaN when the input doesn't begin with a parseable number — as specified in Feature 1 (to_int and to_float) in start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: the screen geometry thing where the available area sometimes drops to zero temporarily
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4.2 (screen_frame_watch) in start.md — the ScreenFrameWatcher that remembers the last non-zero frame and returns it when the live frame collapses to all-zero, as detailed in feature4_2_screen_frame_watch.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the exact algo was agreed on in the architecture call
context_pointers.actual_meaning: 128-bit x64 MurmurHash3 operating on UTF-16 code units, producing a 32-character lowercase hex digest — specified in Feature 2 of start.md and pinned by the test vectors in feature2_murmur_hash.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the availLeft/availTop difference that caused a bug in QA last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The distinction between null and zero for availLeft/availTop: null means the value is absent and must be reported as null in the frame output (top and left positions), while still being treated as 0 for the right/bottom arithmetic — specified in Feature 4.1 and feature4_1_screen_frame.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that in my notes from the planning session. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the architecture notes in start.md — those were the reference documents the team aligned on. If there's still ambiguity after reading those, make a conservative choice that matches the existing test vectors exactly.

feature1_1_to_int.json.description: Parse a textual or numeric input into an integer by reading an optional leading sign and consecutive leading digits, stopping at the first character that is not part of an integer. A fractional part is truncated (not rounded). Input that does not begin with a parseable number yields the not-a-number sentinel.

feature1_2_to_float.json.description: Parse a textual or numeric input into a floating-point number, preserving the fractional part. Input that does not begin with a parseable number yields the not-a-number sentinel.

feature1_3_round.json.description: Round a number to the nearest multiple of a given base (default base 1). The base may be a large integer (rounding to tens, etc.), a small fraction below 1 (rounding to a fixed number of decimals while avoiding floating-point drift), or negative (its magnitude is used). If either the value or the base is the not-a-number sentinel, the result is the not-a-number sentinel.

feature2_murmur_hash.json.description: Compute the 128-bit x64 MurmurHash3 of a UTF-16 string and render it as a fixed 32-character lowercase hexadecimal digest. The same input always produces the same digest; an empty string produces all zeros.

feature3_1_collect_error_isolation.json.description: Collect a set of named data sources into a components map. Each source is invoked independently: a source that returns a value is recorded as a value component, while a source that throws is caught and recorded as an error component whose normalized message carries the source's error text. One failing source never aborts the others, and the collection preserves the original source order.

cases.expected_output:
name=success1 outcome=value value="foo"
name=throwsErrorObject outcome=error message=bar
name=throwsErrorString outcome=error message=baz
name=success2 outcome=value value="baq"

feature3_2_collect_exclusion.json.description: Collect named data sources while skipping any source listed in the exclusion set. Excluded sources are neither invoked nor present in the resulting components map; only the surviving sources, in their original order, are collected.

feature4_1_screen_frame.json.description: Compute the screen 'frame' (the gap on each side between the full screen and the available screen area) as a four-element list ordered top, right, bottom, left. Top equals availTop; right equals width - availWidth - availLeft; bottom equals height - availHeight - availTop; left equals availLeft. When availLeft/availTop are unavailable they are treated as 0 for the width/height arithmetic but reported as the null sentinel in their own positions. A frame whose sides are all zero is reported as all zeros. The rounded form snaps each non-null side to the nearest multiple of 10.

feature4_2_screen_frame_watch.json.description: Watch the screen frame over time to mitigate the fact that the frame momentarily collapses to all-zero in some conditions. A 'watch' step starts monitoring; each 'tick' re-samples the current screen geometry; each 'get' reports the frame that would be exposed. Behavior: while the live frame is all-zero and no non-zero frame has ever been observed, get reports all zeros. Once any non-zero frame is observed it is remembered, and a later get that finds the live frame collapsed to all-zero returns the remembered non-zero frame instead. When the live frame is itself non-zero, get returns the live frame and updates the remembered value.

feature5_device_memory.json.description: Read the advertised device memory (in gigabytes) and normalize it to a number. Numeric and numeric-string inputs are coerced to a number; a missing/undefined reading is reported as the 'unknown' sentinel.

feature6_touch_points.json.description: Read the maximum number of simultaneous touch points and normalize it to an integer, even when the underlying value is reported as a numeric string by some environments.
